change_description: multi-currency support: Currency property on Wallet and Transaction, cross-currency transfer rejection

Cross-currency transfer rejection rule; Currency passed to all CreateTransaction calls; currency read from wallet entity
Currency property added to the ORM entity which also serves as the business object in layered — blurs boundary between data shape and domain rule
Currency property added to the ORM entity which also serves as the business object

Currency field added to TransactionResponse record
Currency threaded from request to manager call and into response mapping

business_logic_changes.reason: Currency property, validation in Wallet.Create, EnsureSameCurrency() domain method encapsulating the cross-currency rule, Restore updated
Currency property, Transaction.Create and Restore signatures updated to carry currency
CreateWalletAsync contract updated with currency parameter — interface is part of the domain boundary
EnsureSameCurrency called before transfer; currency passed to Transaction.Create for all operation types

Currency column added to ORM entity

analysis.notes: Hexagonal touches 5 more files (15 vs 10) because of its strict layer separation: the domain model, the port interface, both infrastructure ORM entities, and both mappers are all distinct files. In the layered design, the ORM entities ARE the business objects, so no separate domain model or mapper files exist — the currency field is added once to an entity that serves both roles. However, this means the layered entity boundary blurs: DataAccess entities had to change to satisfy a business rule, confirming the M1 coupling finding from H1. The extra hexagonal files are all purely mechanical (ORM entities, mappers, DTOs) — the actual business logic change (EnsureSameCurrency) is cleanly isolated to the domain model and services, with zero changes required in Infrastructure. In Layered, the currency mismatch rule required modifying TransactionManager which directly reads Currency from the DataAccess entity.
